topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink bled across the paper, a damning indictment of weeks of effort. A C-minus. It felt like a physical blow, knocking the air from my lungs. I stared at the mark, the edges of the page blurring into a meaningless smear. My hands, usually steady when writing, trembled so violently I had to grip the desk edge to keep them from shaking entirely. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, a noise more animal than human. I didn't move to crumple the paper; instead, I just sat, sinking into the hard plastic chair until the wood dug into my spine. The silence in the room was immense, filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs. Every memory of the late nights fueled by caffeine and sheer stubbornness suddenly felt tainted, cheapened by this single, ugly verdict.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," I whispered, the words barely audible over the hum of the fluorescent lights. I reread the feedback, my eyes skipping over the analytical points and latching onto the final, damning numerical assessment. A hollow ache settled deep in my chest, a cold, heavy pressure that made breathing shallow. I stood up abruptly, needing to escape the confines of the study carrel, but my legs felt disconnected, weighted with lead. I walked to the window, watching the indifferent rush of traffic below. Tears pricked at the corners of my eyes, hot and immediate, blurring the cityscape into streaks of meaningless color. I pressed the heels of my palms against my temples, trying to physically squeeze the pain out of my skull, but the only thing I managed was a slow, hitching exhale, a sound of utter depletion.

[story 3]

The sheer weight of the discrepancy between what I believed I had achieved and what the grade reflected was crushing. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool and slick in my fingers, and simply let it drop onto the desk. The sound was small, insignificant, yet it felt monumental. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. I began pacing the small space between my desk and the bookshelf, a restless, jerky motion that served no purpose other than to expend the excess energy churning inside me. Each step was heavy, each turn of the head bringing the stark reality of the poor score back into sharp focus. I stopped abruptly by the window again, leaning my forehead against the cool glass, letting the sting behind my eyes finally break through, hot and undeniable.
